summary: Gruber issues a correction to his previous claim that KDE and GNOME desktop environments for Linux were incompatible. A reader explained that while they are different desktops with different toolkits, users can run apps from both environments simultaneously. Gruber accepts the correction gracefully but maintains that his broader point still stands: even with cross-desktop compatibility, Linux remains an unattractive market for major commercial software companies like Quark and Adobe.
key_points: KDE and GNOME apps can run alongside each other, similar to how Java Swing apps can run on OS X despite not being native
Gruber acknowledges the factual error while maintaining his broader argument about Linux's commercial viability
Cross-desktop compatibility on Linux doesn't solve the fundamental problem of attracting major commercial software vendors
money_quotes: It's also beside the point I was trying to make.
It's good for everyone that apps written for KDE can run under GNOME and vice versa, but that still doesn't make Linux an attractive market for commercial software companies like Quark and Adobe.
key_insight: Technical interoperability between Linux desktop environments doesn't address the fundamental problem of Linux's unattractiveness as a commercial software market.
